planting.note: Plant container or balled-and-burlapped trees in late winter or early spring in moist to wet, slightly acidic, well-drained to periodically wet soil in full sun to partial shade; Heritage is particularly suited to rain gardens, stream banks, and other moist sites where most trees struggle.

directions: Plant in moist to wet, slightly acidic soil in full sun to partial shade; Heritage is the premier named cultivar of river birch — introduced by Earl Cully of Heritage Tree Nursery in Jacksonville, Illinois, and selected for outstanding bark exfoliation quality, larger leaf size, and superior heat tolerance compared to unselected species plants. The spectacular bark display is the tree's signature attribute: the outer bark exfoliates in large, curling, papery sheets throughout the year, revealing the inner bark in a complex palette of salmon-pink, cream, cinnamon-brown, and reddish-tan tones — a year-round ornamental effect that is one of the finest bark displays of any tree in cultivation. In winter when the tree is bare of leaves, the multi-trunked form with its layered, peeling, multi-colored bark is truly spectacular against a winter sky. Heritage is also one of the most heat-tolerant birches — unlike white or paper birch that decline in the heat of zone 7 and warmer, Heritage river birch thrives through zone 9 in moist to wet sites, making it the birch of choice for the Gulf South. It is highly resistant to bronze birch borer, which devastates most white birch species in the eastern United States. The naturally multi-trunked form and year-round bark display make Heritage an exceptional specimen and focal-point tree.

common_name: Little Bunny Fountain Grass (Miniature Fountain Grass)

planting.note: Plant container divisions in spring in well-drained, lean to moderately fertile, slightly acidic to neutral soil in full sun; Little Bunny is the smallest and most compact fountain grass commonly available — excellent for container gardens, small borders, and edging where even the compact Hameln would be too large.

directions: Plant in well-drained, lean to moderately fertile, slightly acidic to neutral soil in full sun; Little Bunny is the most compact Pennisetum alopecuroides selection available — a true miniature fountain grass reaching only 12–18 inches in both height and spread, compared to the 24–36 inch Hameln and 36–48 inch standard species. Despite its tiny size, Little Bunny produces the characteristic fountain grass form in perfect miniature: the fine-bladed, arching, clumping foliage topped by small, fluffy, white to cream-colored bottle-brush plumes that sway gracefully with the slightest breeze. The diminutive scale makes it perfect for the front edge of borders, rock gardens, container plantings, and formal edging uses where even the most compact standard fountain grass selections would be oversized. In zone 9, Little Bunny blooms in late summer (August–September) and the plumes persist attractively through winter. Like all Pennisetum alopecuroides selections, Little Bunny is a warm-season grass that emerges late in spring, grows actively through summer, flowers in late summer, and is cut back to 4–6 inches in late winter (February in zone 9) before new growth begins. Very drought-tolerant once established; essentially maintenance-free except for annual cutback. Low-seeding — less invasive spreading than some fountain grass types.

common_name: Golden Tiara Hosta (Small Gold-Margined Green Hosta)

planting.note: Plant divisions or container plants in spring in moist, well-drained, slightly acidic to neutral, humus-rich soil in partial to full shade; Golden Tiara is one of the faster-growing hostas and reaches presentable size within 2–3 years of planting — suitable for edging and mass planting where quick establishment is needed.

directions: Plant in moist, well-drained, slightly acidic to neutral, humus-rich soil in partial to full shade; Golden Tiara is one of the most widely planted small hostas in American horticulture — a compact, fast-growing cultivar producing neat mounds of heart-shaped, medium-green leaves edged with a thin but distinct golden-yellow to chartreuse margin. At 12–18 inches tall and 18–24 inches wide, Golden Tiara is well-scaled for edging shade beds, mass planting as ground cover under trees, and container gardens where larger hostas would overwhelm the space. Golden Tiara is one of the faster-maturing hostas — reaching attractive size in 2–3 years rather than the 5–7 years required by large-growing hostas like Frances Williams. The thin golden margin brightens the leaf edge and catches light in shadowy conditions, creating subtle foliage interest throughout the season. In summer, tall, slender scapes bear purple, funnel-shaped flowers that are mildly fragrant — Golden Tiara is one of the better hostas for flower production relative to its compact size. The compact habit, rapid establishment, and adaptable nature make Golden Tiara an excellent hosta for beginning gardeners who want the lush, tropical effect of hostas without the wait required by large-growing selections. Slug damage is the primary cultural problem; iron phosphate bait or diatomaceous earth applied around the crown helps manage slugs in humid environments.

common_name: June Gold Peach (Early Low-Chill Freestone Peach)

planting.note: Plant bare-root or container trees in late winter (January–February in zone 9) in well-drained, slightly acidic, fertile, deep soil in full sun; June Gold requires approximately 650 chill hours and is well-suited to zone 8–9 conditions with consistent chill hour accumulation.

directions: Plant in well-drained, slightly acidic (pH 6.0–6.5), fertile, deep soil in full sun; June Gold is an early-season freestone peach cultivar that ripens in late May to early June in zone 9 — one of the earliest-ripening freestone peach selections available for Gulf Coast production. The medium-large, round fruit has yellow flesh with red coloring and a rich, full peach flavor with good sweetness. The early ripening season avoids the peak summer disease pressure (brown rot, oriental fruit moth) that damages later-ripening cultivars, and it extends the Gulf South peach season earlier. June Gold requires approximately 650 chill hours — reliably accumulated in zone 9a and most zone 9 climates during typical winters, and less frequently limiting than the 800–1000 hours required by standard northern peach cultivars like Elberta and Redhaven. The tree is vigorous and productive with proper annual pruning to an open-vase form. In zone 9, peaches are planted in January–February for the spring growing season, and the orchard management cycle includes dormant oil and copper sprays at bud swell, thinning to 6–8 inches between fruits after natural drop, and a preventive brown rot spray program as harvest approaches.

planting.note: Plant divisions or container plants in spring in moist, well-drained, slightly acidic to neutral, humus-rich soil in partial to full shade; Wide Brim's broad cream to white margin is brightest and most attractive in partial shade with some morning light — deep shade may reduce the vividness of the contrast.

directions: Plant in moist, well-drained, slightly acidic to neutral, humus-rich soil in partial to full shade; Wide Brim is a striking medium-sized hosta producing broadly oval to heart-shaped leaves with a deep olive-green center surrounded by a wide, irregular cream to creamy-white margin — one of the boldest and most clearly defined white-margined hostas with a broad, contrasting edge that creates a dramatic two-tone foliage effect. The wide creamy-white margin (often 1–2 inches wide on mature leaves) against the deep green center provides a luminous brightness in shadowy garden settings. Wide Brim is a medium-large hosta reaching 18–24 inches tall and 30–42 inches wide at maturity with an arching, mounding habit and good sun tolerance for a cream-to-white variegated hosta — most cream-margined hostas are more susceptible to leaf scorch in brighter conditions, but Wide Brim handles partial shade locations with some morning sun relatively well. Pale lavender flowers appear on tall scapes in mid-summer. The white-margined foliage brightens dark shade conditions and provides maximum contrast with deep green or dark-leafed companion plants. Like all hostas, slug management is important — regular monitoring and iron phosphate bait applications prevent the unsightly slug damage that can disfigure an otherwise beautiful hosta specimen.
